skill.name: snowtower-admin
skill.description: Administration guide for SnowTower teams managing SnowDDL deployments, Snowflake users, role grants, CI/CD workflows, troubleshooting, and security operations.
skill.summary: Manage SnowTower and Snowflake administration workflows with deployment, access, and operations guidance.
skill.author: Database-Tycoon

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and secret patterns in SKILL.md. Review shows these are mostly markdown examples in an administrator guide, but the skill still carries elevated operational risk because it instructs privileged Snowflake deployments, user administration, and credential handling.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Snowflake Administration Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill documents commands for SnowDDL deployment, user management, security checks, warehouse suspension, and service account reset. These commands match the stated administrator purpose, but incorrect use could alter production infrastructure, grants, or access controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and administrative in scope, including deployment, security audit, warehouse control, and account reset actions. The context is legitimate operations guidance, so this is elevated operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Private Key Handling Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill references Snowflake environment values, a private key path under .ssh, Fernet keys, and uploading a private key to GitHub secrets. This is expected for Snowflake administration, but it requires careful secret handling and access control.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced values are clearly credentials or credential paths used for Snowflake access. No exfiltration endpoint is present, but mishandling these instructions could expose sensitive material.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are markdown code fences or inline command examples, not executable Ruby backticks. The Windows SAM, ransomware extension, weak crypto, and role-token detections also appear to be substring matches in administrative text or placeholder values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context shows markdown examples and Snowflake terminology rather than executable code or malware indicators. No prompt injection language or hidden instruction to bypass review was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Example Network Policy Addresses
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP findings are example CIDR ranges in a Snowflake network policy. One range is documentation-only and the other is private address space, with no data transfer or external callback behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses appear inside a YAML example for allowed_ip_list. There is no code path that connects to these addresses or sends data to them.

dangerous_patterns.title: High-Impact Deployment and Access Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill guides users through deployment, role, user, warehouse, and account reset operations. These are not malicious, but they should only be used by authorized administrators with change review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete and can affect Snowflake infrastructure. Their placement in an administrator guide makes legitimate use likely, but operational impact is significant.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Configuration References
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs users to configure Snowflake account, role, private key path, and Fernet key values. These references are necessary for administration but increase sensitivity of the workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment values include secret-related configuration. The file does not disclose real secrets, but the instructions touch sensitive material.

content.value_statement: SnowTower administration involves many high-impact SnowDDL and Snowflake workflows. This skill organizes deployment, access, security, CI/CD, and troubleshooting guidance for authorized operators.
actual_capabilities: Guides SnowDDL plan and deploy-safe workflows for infrastructure changes.
Explains Snowflake user creation, service accounts, MFA checks, and RSA key setup.
Documents business role, technical role, and schema role naming patterns.
Provides CI/CD steps for branches, validation, pull requests, tags, and releases.
Covers troubleshooting for schema grant drift, authentication failures, and locked users.
Summarizes warehouse status, cost analysis, emergency suspension, and health checks.
limitations: Requires an existing SnowTower repository with the referenced uv commands available.
Assumes authorized Snowflake administrator access and valid local credentials.
Does not execute changes automatically or verify live Snowflake state by itself.
Examples may need adaptation for each organization, account, and branch model.

use_cases.title: Deploy SnowDDL Changes
use_cases.description: Preview, review, and apply SnowDDL changes while preserving schema grants and reducing deployment mistakes.

use_cases.title: Provision Secure Access
use_cases.description: Create users, assign roles, check MFA status, and manage service account authentication through documented workflows.

use_cases.title: Resolve Production Issues
use_cases.description: Investigate authentication failures, locked users, schema drift, warehouse status, and emergency rollback steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Simple Deployment
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a small YAML change
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me prepare a SnowTower deployment for one user YAML change. List the preview, review, and apply steps I should follow.

prompt_templates.title: Create a New User
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through adding a Snowflake PERSON user with a default business role, warehouse, email, and authentication settings.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate Grant Drift
prompt_templates.prompt: A SnowDDL plan shows many schema grant revokes. Help me determine whether this is drift and how to deploy safely.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Emergency Recovery
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me build an emergency SnowTower checklist for rollback, service account key reset, warehouse suspension, and audit follow-up.

output_examples.input: I need to add a new analyst user safely.
output_examples.output: The assistant provides a staged checklist: choose PERSON type, define the business role, add user settings, run a plan, review role grants, and deploy with the safe wrapper after approval.

output_examples.input: The plan shows many schema grant revokes.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains likely schema grant drift, highlights risky revoke statements, recommends reviewing the plan, and points to the safe deployment and schema grant restore workflow.

output_examples.input: A service account key needs rotation.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines key regeneration, Snowflake public key update, secret store update, validation, and documentation steps without exposing private key material.

best_practices: Always run and review a SnowDDL plan before applying infrastructure changes.
Use approved change control for user, role, warehouse, and secret updates.
Keep private keys, Fernet keys, and Snowflake credentials out of chat and logs.
anti_patterns: Do not apply deployments when the plan contains unexplained drops or mass revokes.
Do not use emergency access accounts for routine administration.
Do not paste real private keys, passwords, or account secrets into prompts.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: It is intended for authorized SnowTower, SnowDDL, Snowflake, security, DevOps, and on-call administrators.

faq.question: Does the skill run Snowflake commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command examples. The operator remains responsible for execution and approval.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it guides privileged infrastructure and credential workflows that can affect production systems.

faq.question: Can it help with user provisioning?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes interactive, direct YAML, and non-interactive user creation workflows.

faq.question: Can it help troubleshoot SnowDDL plan output?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains common plan signals, schema grant drift, authentication failures, and locked user checks.

faq.question: What should not be shared with the assistant?
faq.answer: Do not share real private keys, passwords, Fernet keys, GitHub secrets, or sensitive account identifiers.
